attributes.stem:
A 30-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-day history of an itchy, mildly burning feeling rash on her trunk and thighs as well as some mild fatigue and low-grade fever. She reports that she tried a new spa that had just opened in her neighborhood. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, she appears well though slightly tired, with vital signs within normal limits. There are multiple erythematous papules and pustules distributed across her trunk and thighs, particularly in areas that were covered by her swimming costume. The lesions are tender to touch but there is no surrounding cellulitis.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a pseudomonas folliculitis (spa pool or hot tub folliculitis) caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa. The history of hot tub exposure followed by the development of a pustular rash in a bathing suit distribution is pathognomonic. The rash can be mildly pruiritic and burning, and the patient may have nausea, fatigue or headache. The condition is typically self-limiting in immunocompetent individuals.

Supportive care only is the most appropriate management as the condition typically resolves spontaneously within 7-10 days. Symptomatic relief with cool compresses, simple analgesics and over-the-counter antihistamines for pruritis may be helpful.

Oral ciprofloxacin would be excessive for this self-limiting condition. Systemic antibiotics are only indicated in severe cases, immunocompromised patients, or when there is evidence of spreading infection.

Topical mupirocin would not be effective against Pseudomonas aeruginosa and is unnecessary given the self-limiting nature of the condition.

Oral flucloxacillin would not be appropriate as it does not provide coverage against Pseudomonas aeruginosa. It is primarily used for staphylococcal and streptococcal infections.

Topical betamethasone is not indicated and could potentially worsen the condition by suppressing local immune responses. Steroids are not recommended for infectious processes.

Patient education about prevention is important, including avoiding poorly maintained hot tubs and ensuring proper chlorination and pH levels in swimming pools and spas. The patient should be advised that the condition is not contagious person-to-person.
learningPoints: Pseudomonas folliculitis typically develops 48-72 hours after exposure to contaminated water in hot tubs or swimming pools.
The condition is self-limiting and usually resolves within 7-10 days without specific treatment.
references: Oakley A, Al-Ani Z, Jarrett P, Mitchell G. Spa pool folliculitis. DermNet NZ. Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/spa-pool-folliculitis
Jacob JS, Tschen J. Hot Tub-Associated Pseudomonas Folliculitis: A Case Report and Review of Host Risk Factors. Cureus. 2020. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7584313/
